title.emphasis: 2. The AI Authorship Analogy: Who Holds Creative Control?

section.para: The U.S. Copyright Office (USCO) has recently issued guidance on the copyrightability of works created with the assistance of generative AI. The central principle of this guidance is that copyright protection depends on the degree of human creative control over the AI's output.3 A mere text prompt is considered an unprotectable "idea." For a human to be the "author" of an AI-generated image, they must demonstrate that they exercised sufficient creative control over the expressive elements of the final work.3
This framework provides a powerful analogy for the social media context, but with the roles inverted.

listitem.para: The User's Contribution: The human user provides the initial, original work of authorship—the text, photo, or video. This is the core expressive act.

The Platform's Contribution: The platform's AI (its moderation and algorithmic amplification systems) then exercises the final, decisive "creative control" over that work. It determines the final form of the work that is actually "published" to the public, whether it is displayed prominently, suppressed, or removed entirely.

section.para: If the USCO's standard for authorship is "intellectual domination" over the final expressive output, then it can be argued that the platform, through its AI, is asserting a form of co-authorship or "executive authorship" over the collective work of its user base.3 The platform is not merely hosting content; its AI is actively arranging, transforming, and curating a vast compilation of user submissions into a monetizable commercial product. This mechanized curation is a creative act of selection and arrangement, which is itself a copyrightable form of expression.
